summary: Notes from David Silver's 2015 Reinforcement Learning course at UCL, covering foundational concepts such as Markov Decision Processes, model-free prediction and control, value function approximation, policy gradients, and the exploration-exploitation trade-off.

principles.statement: Model-based reinforcement learning is fundamentally limited by the accuracy of the learned model.
principles.rationale: Because model-based RL relies on simulated experience to plan the value function or policy, any approximation errors in the model will compound and degrade the resulting policy.
principles.source_quote: Model-based RL is only as good as the estimated model

principles.statement: Stochastic policies can outperform deterministic policies in environments with state aliasing.
principles.rationale: When an agent cannot fully observe the true state (aliasing), a deterministic policy might get stuck making consistently wrong choices, whereas a stochastic policy can break symmetry and perform better.
principles.source_quote: Whenever state aliasing occurs a stochastic policy can do better than a deterministic policy

frameworks.name: Deep Q-Networks (DQN)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When approximating value functions in reinforcement learning using neural networks to ensure stable learning.
steps: Take action a_t according to epsilon-greedy policy
Store transition (s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1}) in replay memory D
Sample random mini-batch of transition (s, a, r, s') from D
Compute Q-learning targets with respect to fixed parameters w^-
Optimize MSE between Q-network and Q-learning targets
frameworks.source_quote: DQN uses experience replay and fixed Q-targets

frameworks.name: Dyna
frameworks.when_to_apply: When integrating learning and planning to maximize data efficiency from both real and simulated environments.
steps: Learn a model from real experience
Learn and plan value function (and/or policy) from real and simulated experience

frameworks.name: Actor-Critic Algorithm
frameworks.when_to_apply: When using policy gradient methods and needing to reduce the high variance of evaluating a policy.
steps: Critic: Updates action-value function parameters w
Actor: Updates policy parameter theta, in the direction suggested by the critic

mental_models.name: On-policy vs. Off-policy learning
mental_models.description: A metaphor for understanding how agents learn from experience. On-policy is 'learning on the job' (learning from the actions you are currently taking), while off-policy is 'looking over someone's shoulder' (learning from the actions of another agent or a past policy).

mental_models.name: Optimism in the face of uncertainty
mental_models.description: An approach to the exploration-exploitation dilemma where the agent assumes unknown states or actions have high value, naturally driving exploration of unexplored areas.
mental_models.example: Probability matching and the UCB1 algorithm.

heuristics: Assign credit to the most frequent states (frequency heuristic) and the most recent states (recency heuristic) using eligibility traces.
Reduce variance in policy gradients by subtracting a baseline function, such as the state value function.

signature_quotes.text: Monte-Carlo learning is a really bad idea off-policy, it does not work because over many steps, your target policy and your behavior policy never match enough to be useful
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why Monte-Carlo methods fail for off-policy control.

signature_quotes.text: Model-based RL is only as good as the estimated model
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the disadvantages and limitations of model-based reinforcement learning.

signature_quotes.text: Whenever state aliasing occurs a stochastic policy can do better than a deterministic policy
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the advantages of policy-based RL over value-based RL.

anti_patterns.description: Using Monte-Carlo learning for off-policy control.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Over many steps, the target policy and the behavior policy diverge too much, meaning they 'never match enough to be useful.'
